---
title: "Live-CD won´t detect my HDD[SOLVED]"
date: 2016-01-12
forum: Installation &amp; Upgrades
---

### Post by jkuwajima on 2016-01-12
Hi, Ububtu users.
I
I´ve been trying to dual-boot (Windows 7/ Ubuntu 14.04.LTS) for a while, and still haven´t been able even to detect my HDD. I already changed BIOS settings to AHCI and UEFI. It is really curious because the installations will not see anything, but using GParted we can clearly see all partitions. I don´t know what else to do. By the way my laptop is a Dell Vostro 3560...

Here some pictures from my problem. Hopefully I´ll be able to solve it!

[IMG]http://i.stack.imgur.com/i2ajl.jpg[/IMG]


[IMG]http://i.stack.imgur.com/fk8Up.png[/IMG]


Hi, guys after using FixParts. I manage to make the live-CD detect my HDD....Look my comments below for a little bit more details.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2016-01-12
If the motherboard has a UEFI boot system and Windows 7 is installed in BIOS/Legacy/Compatibility mode (very likely) then Ubuntu will need to be installed in BIOS/Legacy mode. That means the live/install session has to be run in BIOS/Legacy mode.

Are you by any chance loading the Ubuntu install disk in EFI mode? What install option did you choose? Install alongside? Was that option offered? Or did you choose Something Else? In which case there should be unallocated space for the installer to create 2 partitions for Ubuntu. And there is not. The partition that is labelled Ubuntu is an ntfs formatted partition which the Ubuntu installer cannot install into.

These are my guesses as to why the installer is not offering you a partitions to install to install Ubuntu into.

Regards.

---

### Post by yancek on 2016-01-12
If you add up the size of the different partitions and compare that to the size of the drive, they are pretty much equal.  That means there is no space on which to install Ubuntu.  I notice also that sda4 is an ntfs filesystem and for some reason shows Ubuntu under the Label columns.  You can install Ubuntu on an ntfs partition but it won't run.  If you want to use sda4 for Ubuntu, you need to select the "Something Else" option mentioned above and format it to a Linux filesystem.

### Post by yancek on 2016-01-12
Have you done md5 checksums on the downloaded iso files of the different Linux distributions you have tried to verify the download?  I would do that first from windows. I would also suggest that you delete sda4 and leave it as unallocated space.  The fact that all your partitions are windows might be at least part of the problem.  You can create and format a partition with the installer but you can not do that from windows.

---

### Post by Geoffrey_Arndt on 2016-01-12
Do two things . . . 

>   Make room for Ubuntu using Windows (not the installer disk) . . . by de-fragging windows, then by using the Windows disk tool to repartition the disk. (don't use gparted for that . . yet),
>   After you have sufficient unallocated space, you can optionally use gparted to create an "ext4" partition for Ubuntu,
>   Be very sure to turn off fast start/hibernation (as that will also ruin a good install if Windows is hibernated versus shut down completely,
>   You shouldn't install Ubuntu in legacy mode.
>   Check the boxes at install to get the restricted or proprietary drivers (I forget the exact wording) - - be sure to have a good, stable internet connection,
>   Use the "Something Else" install option (not the "install alongside")
> Do NOT delete or remove your Windows partitions (like sda1, sda2, sda3, sda4).

Good luck.

---

### Post by jkuwajima on 2016-01-14
Hi Grahammechanical, Yancek and Geoffrey_Arndt! Thank you very much for your support I managed to fix it.

In the end all this Bios tunning didn't played any role on my problem, as Grahammechanichal suggested I set my bios back to legacy boot and managed to install using a tool called [FixParts]("http://www.rodsbooks.com/fixparts/"). Aparently the problem is related to GPT data.

1)So the first step was to boot using your USB Stack or CD/DVD and select Try Ubuntu.
2) After doing that just load terminal > ctrl+alt+t
3) And launch FixParts to fix it...it can correct the problems almost automatically; [URL="http://askubuntu.com/questions/343768/why-is-it-not-showing-any-partitions"]see its Web page for details:
[/URL]
Thanks again to all that helped me!

---
